text: In this case, we are going to describe laparoscopic myomectomy for a patient having a hemoglobin of five point six and a large posterior fibroid measuring approximately eight by eight centimeters.

Following the infiltration of vasopressin, the surgeon makes an incision along the most prominent aspect of the large posterior fibroid and its capsule. The uterus is stabilized, and a Bissinger bipolar device is used to perform the dissection, maintaining a bloodless surgical plane.
The surgeon uses traction and counter traction to completely enucleate the fibroid, depositing it into the right iliac fossa to clear the surgical field for laparoscopic suturing.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic suturing using a quill suture, characterized by its self-retaining barb design with vertically aligned spikes. The suturing is executed in a multi-layered fashion to secure the tissue.

The surgeon begins laparoscopic suturing by addressing the under layer to obliterate the depth of the base of the myoma cavity. After the cavity is obliterated, the surgeon continues with a baseball type suture technique to complete the suturing process.

The surgeon overlaps the walls of the cut end of the fibroid capsule, minimizing exposure to the peritoneum. The quill suture is embedded away from the margin of the large intestine and other adherent structures, aiming to reduce post-operative pain and adhesions.

The surgeon completes the suturing of the myometrium using a baseball stitch technique, ensuring that the layers of myometrium are deposited on top of each other. This arrangement minimizes the formation of intramyometrial hematomas and enhances the strength of the sutured tissue.
The surgeon utilizes three laparoscopic ports: two lateral ports and a central suprapubic port. The central port is used for morcellation to extract the fibroid from the peritoneal cavity.
The surgeon uses three needle holders during the procedure: one to stabilize the edge of the myoma and maintain the uterus's position, while the other two maneuver the needle through the myoma bed.

The surgeon performs laparoscopic suturing over the myoma bed with gentle and quick movements, aiming to complete the suturing before the vasopressin effect diminishes to minimize blood loss during the procedure.

It is also important to remember that when we are using a barbed suture or a quill suture, laparoscopic suturing can be done without any knotting. And because it is done without any knotting, it also helps us in saving significant amount of time.
At the end of the myomectomy, the surgeon assesses the placement of the needles. If the needles are widely apart, suturing them together is not performed, maintaining a bloodless plane of dissection.
